\subsection{Prerequisites}
\label{\detokenize{deployment/index:prerequisites}}\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Docker} (via Docker Desktop or Rancher Desktop)

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Python 3.14+} (for local development outside containers)

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{uv} package manager (\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{curl \sphinxhyphen{}LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh}})

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{make} (standard on macOS/Linux)

\end{itemize}

\sphinxstepscope


\chapter{Developer Guide}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/index:developer-guide}}\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/index::doc}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Information for developers contributing to PyCypher.

\sphinxstepscope


\section{Architecture}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/architecture:architecture}}\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/architecture::doc}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Overview of the PyCypher architecture and design principles.


\subsection{Project Structure}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/architecture:project-structure}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
This is a \sphinxstylestrong{monorepo workspace} containing three interdependent packages:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
pycypher\PYGZhy{}nmetl/
├── packages/
│   ├── shared/         \PYGZsh{} Common utilities and logging
│   ├── pycypher/       \PYGZsh{} Cypher parser, AST, and BindingFrame execution engine
│   └── fastopendata/   \PYGZsh{} ETL pipeline for open data (Census, TIGER, OSM)
├── tests/              \PYGZsh{} Test suite
├── docs/               \PYGZsh{} Documentation
└── pyproject.toml      \PYGZsh{} Workspace configuration
\end{sphinxVerbatim}


\subsubsection{Dependency Order}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/architecture:dependency-order}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Packages depend on each other in this order:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
shared → pycypher → fastopendata
\end{sphinxVerbatim}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{shared} has no dependencies on other packages

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{pycypher} depends on \sphinxstylestrong{shared}

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{fastopendata} depends on \sphinxstylestrong{shared} and \sphinxstylestrong{pycypher}

\end{itemize}


\subsection{PyCypher Architecture}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/architecture:pycypher-architecture}}

\subsubsection{Core Components}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/architecture:core-components}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Grammar Parser}

\sphinxAtStartPar
Uses Lark with the Earley algorithm for maximum grammar expressiveness.  The
compiled parser is cached as a process\sphinxhyphen{}level singleton via
{\hyperref[\detokenize{api/pycypher:pycypher.grammar_parser.get_default_parser}]{\sphinxcrossref{\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{get\_default\_parser()}}}}}; do not construct
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{GrammarParser()}} directly in application code:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{k+kn}{from}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{n+nn}{pycypher}\PYG{n+nn}{.}\PYG{n+nn}{grammar\PYGZus{}parser}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{get\PYGZus{}default\PYGZus{}parser}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Returns the cached singleton — safe to call repeatedly}
\PYG{n}{parser} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{get\PYGZus{}default\PYGZus{}parser}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{n}{raw\PYGZus{}ast} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{parser}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{parse\PYGZus{}to\PYGZus{}ast}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{query}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Grammar Rule Mixins} (modular transformer architecture)

\sphinxAtStartPar
Grammar transformation rules are organized into five focused mixin modules
under \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{pycypher.grammar\_rule\_mixins/}}:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
grammar\PYGZus{}rule\PYGZus{}mixins/
├── \PYGZus{}\PYGZus{}init\PYGZus{}\PYGZus{}.py       \PYGZsh{} Re\PYGZhy{}exports all 5 mixins
├── literals.py       \PYGZsh{} LiteralRulesMixin — numbers, strings, booleans, null, lists, maps
├── expressions.py    \PYGZsh{} ExpressionRulesMixin — boolean, comparison, arithmetic, string ops
├── patterns.py       \PYGZsh{} PatternRulesMixin — node/relationship patterns, labels, properties
├── functions.py      \PYGZsh{} FunctionRulesMixin — function calls, CASE, comprehensions, REDUCE
└── clauses.py        \PYGZsh{} ClauseRulesMixin — MATCH, RETURN, WITH, SET, DELETE, CREATE, MERGE
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{CompositeTransformer}} (in \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{grammar\_transformers.py}}) orchestrates these
mixins with method\sphinxhyphen{}resolution caching for thread\sphinxhyphen{}safe concurrent AST warmup.

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{AST Models}

\sphinxAtStartPar
Pydantic\sphinxhyphen{}based strongly\sphinxhyphen{}typed AST nodes:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{k+kn}{from}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{n+nn}{pycypher}\PYG{n+nn}{.}\PYG{n+nn}{ast\PYGZus{}models}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{p}{(}
    \PYG{n}{Query}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{Match}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{Return}\PYG{p}{,}
    \PYG{n}{NodePattern}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{Variable}\PYG{p}{,}
    \PYG{n}{ASTConverter}
\PYG{p}{)}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} All nodes are Pydantic models}
\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} \PYGZhy{} Automatic validation}
\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} \PYGZhy{} Type safety}
\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} \PYGZhy{} Serialization support}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{BindingFrame IR}

\sphinxAtStartPar
The core intermediate representation for query execution:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{k+kn}{from}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{n+nn}{pycypher}\PYG{n+nn}{.}\PYG{n+nn}{binding\PYGZus{}frame}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{BindingFrame}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{EntityScan}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{BindingFilter}
\PYG{k+kn}{from}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{n+nn}{pycypher}\PYG{n+nn}{.}\PYG{n+nn}{binding\PYGZus{}evaluator}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{BindingExpressionEvaluator}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} BindingFrame: DataFrame whose columns ARE Cypher variable names}
\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} No column prefixing, no hash IDs, no variable\PYGZus{}map threading}
\PYG{n}{scan} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{EntityScan}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{entity\PYGZus{}type}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{Person}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{var\PYGZus{}name}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{p}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{n}{frame} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{scan}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{scan}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{context}\PYG{p}{)}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Properties fetched on demand — never stored in the frame}
\PYG{n}{names} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{frame}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{get\PYGZus{}property}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{p}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{name}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Relational Models}

\sphinxAtStartPar
Data containers for entity and relationship tables:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{k+kn}{from}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{n+nn}{pycypher}\PYG{n+nn}{.}\PYG{n+nn}{relational\PYGZus{}models}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{p}{(}
    \PYG{n}{EntityTable}\PYG{p}{,}        \PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Holds entity IDs + attributes}
    \PYG{n}{RelationshipTable}\PYG{p}{,}  \PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Holds relationship IDs + source/target}
    \PYG{n}{Context}\PYG{p}{,}            \PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Holds all tables + registered functions}
\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}


\subsection{Design Patterns}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/architecture:design-patterns}}

\subsubsection{Visitor Pattern}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/architecture:visitor-pattern}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
AST traversal uses visitor pattern:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{k}{class}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{n+nc}{MyVisitor}\PYG{p}{:}
    \PYG{k}{def}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{n+nf}{visit\PYGZus{}Match}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n+nb+bp}{self}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{node}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{p}{:}
        \PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Process Match nodes}
        \PYG{k}{pass}

    \PYG{k}{def}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{n+nf}{visit\PYGZus{}Return}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n+nb+bp}{self}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{node}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{p}{:}
        \PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Process Return nodes}
        \PYG{k}{pass}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Used in:}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
AST conversion (raw → typed)

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Validation

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Translation to BindingFrame operations

\end{itemize}


\subsubsection{BindingFrame Execution Model}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/architecture:bindingframe-execution-model}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Variables are column names.  Each row is one possible assignment of entity
IDs to those variables.  Attributes are fetched on demand by ID\sphinxhyphen{}keyed lookup
against the entity table in the \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{Context}}:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} EntityScan: produces single\PYGZhy{}column frame}
\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Person IDs in column \PYGZdq{}p\PYGZdq{}}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} RelationshipScan: produces three\PYGZhy{}column frame}
\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} rel IDs in \PYGZdq{}r\PYGZdq{}, source in \PYGZdq{}\PYGZus{}src\PYGZus{}r\PYGZdq{}, target in \PYGZdq{}\PYGZus{}tgt\PYGZus{}r\PYGZdq{}}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} join(left\PYGZus{}col, right\PYGZus{}col): inner join on the shared structural key}
\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} filter(mask): boolean mask filter}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Benefits over legacy relational algebra:}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
No opaque 32\sphinxhyphen{}hex HASH\_ID column names

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
No \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{EntityType\_\_propertyname}} column names bleeding into operator logic

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
No \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{variable\_map}} metadata threading through every operator

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Variables as column names: the column \sphinxstyleemphasis{is} the variable

\end{itemize}


\subsubsection{Query\sphinxhyphen{}Scoped Atomicity}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/architecture:query-scoped-atomicity}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
SET clause mutations are buffered in a shadow layer on \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{Context}}
and only committed on successful query completion:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{n}{context}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{begin\PYGZus{}query}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}   \PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} initialise shadow layer}
\PYG{k}{try}\PYG{p}{:}
    \PYG{n}{result} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{execute\PYGZus{}inner}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{query}\PYG{p}{)}
    \PYG{n}{context}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{commit\PYGZus{}query}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}    \PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} promote shadows to canonical tables}
    \PYG{k}{return} \PYG{n}{result}
\PYG{k}{except} \PYG{n+ne}{Exception}\PYG{p}{:}
    \PYG{n}{context}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{rollback\PYGZus{}query}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}  \PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} discard all pending mutations}
    \PYG{k}{raise}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
This ensures a failed query never leaves the context in a partially\sphinxhyphen{}mutated
state.


\subsection{Extension Points}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/architecture:extension-points}}

\subsubsection{Custom Cypher Functions}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/architecture:custom-cypher-functions}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Register user\sphinxhyphen{}defined functions via the \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{@context.cypher\_function}} decorator:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{k+kn}{from}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{n+nn}{pycypher}\PYG{n+nn}{.}\PYG{n+nn}{relational\PYGZus{}models}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{Context}

\PYG{n}{context} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{Context}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}

\PYG{n+nd}{@context}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{cypher\PYGZus{}function}
\PYG{k}{def}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{n+nf}{my\PYGZus{}function}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{x}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{p}{:}
    \PYG{k}{return} \PYG{n}{x} \PYG{o}{*} \PYG{l+m+mi}{2}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Now callable as my\PYGZus{}function(n.value) in Cypher queries}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}


\subsubsection{Custom Scalar Functions}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/architecture:custom-scalar-functions}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Register additional scalar functions in the \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{ScalarFunctionRegistry}}:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{k+kn}{from}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{n+nn}{pycypher}\PYG{n+nn}{.}\PYG{n+nn}{scalar\PYGZus{}functions}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{ScalarFunctionRegistry}

\PYG{n}{registry} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{ScalarFunctionRegistry}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{get\PYGZus{}instance}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{n}{registry}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{register\PYGZus{}function}\PYG{p}{(}
    \PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{myFunc}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{k}{lambda} \PYG{n}{s}\PYG{p}{:} \PYG{n}{s}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{apply}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n+nb}{str}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{upper}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{min\PYGZus{}args}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{1}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{max\PYGZus{}args}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{1}\PYG{p}{,}
\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}


\subsection{Testing Architecture}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/architecture:testing-architecture}}

\subsubsection{Test Organization}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/architecture:test-organization}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Tests are co\sphinxhyphen{}located in the \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{tests/}} directory:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
tests/
├── test\PYGZus{}ast\PYGZus{}models.py              \PYGZsh{} AST node tests
├── test\PYGZus{}ast\PYGZus{}models\PYGZus{}coverage\PYGZus{}gaps.py \PYGZsh{} Coverage improvements
├── test\PYGZus{}grammar\PYGZus{}parser.py          \PYGZsh{} Parser tests
├── test\PYGZus{}data\PYGZus{}correctness\PYGZus{}*.py      \PYGZsh{} Data correctness tests
├── test\PYGZus{}semantic\PYGZus{}validator.py      \PYGZsh{} Validation tests
├── test\PYGZus{}binding\PYGZus{}frame.py           \PYGZsh{} BindingFrame IR tests
└── test\PYGZus{}star\PYGZus{}gaps.py               \PYGZsh{} Star execution tests
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Conventions:}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Use pytest fixtures for common test data

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Separate unit tests from integration tests

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Aim for >90\% coverage on new code

\end{itemize}


\subsubsection{Test Execution}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/architecture:test-execution}}
\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Run all tests}
uv\PYG{+w}{ }run\PYG{+w}{ }pytest

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Run with coverage}
uv\PYG{+w}{ }run\PYG{+w}{ }pytest\PYG{+w}{ }\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}cov\PYG{o}{=}pycypher\PYG{+w}{ }\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}cov\PYGZhy{}report\PYG{o}{=}html

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Run specific tests}
uv\PYG{+w}{ }run\PYG{+w}{ }pytest\PYG{+w}{ }tests/test\PYGZus{}ast\PYGZus{}models.py

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Parallel execution}
uv\PYG{+w}{ }run\PYG{+w}{ }pytest\PYG{+w}{ }\PYGZhy{}n\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{l+m}{4}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}


\subsection{Configuration Management}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/architecture:configuration-management}}

\subsubsection{Environment Management}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/architecture:environment-management}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Use \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{uv}} for all Python operations:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
uv\PYG{+w}{ }sync

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Run Python scripts}
uv\PYG{+w}{ }run\PYG{+w}{ }python\PYG{+w}{ }script.py

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Install packages}
uv\PYG{+w}{ }pip\PYG{+w}{ }install\PYG{+w}{ }\PYGZlt{}package\PYGZgt{}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Never use pip directly} \sphinxhyphen{} always use \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{uv pip}} to maintain workspace consistency.


\subsubsection{Type Checking}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/architecture:type-checking}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Use \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{ty}} (not mypy) for type checking:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
uv\PYG{+w}{ }run\PYG{+w}{ }ty\PYG{+w}{ }check
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Requirements:}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
All functions MUST have type annotations

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Python 3.14.0a6+freethreaded required

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Strict type checking enabled

\end{itemize}


\subsubsection{Code Formatting}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/architecture:code-formatting}}
\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Format code}
make\PYG{+w}{ }format\PYG{+w}{  }\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Runs isort + ruff format}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Check without modifying}
ruff\PYG{+w}{ }check
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Configuration:} \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{pyproject.toml}} (\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{{[}tool.ruff{]}}} section) with \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{select = {[}"ALL"{]}}} (highly strict)


\subsection{Build System}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/architecture:build-system}}

\subsubsection{Makefile Targets}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/architecture:makefile-targets}}
\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
make\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{n+nb}{test}\PYG{+w}{      }\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Run test suite}
make\PYG{+w}{ }coverage\PYG{+w}{  }\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Generate coverage report}
make\PYG{+w}{ }format\PYG{+w}{    }\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Format code}
make\PYG{+w}{ }docs\PYG{+w}{      }\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Build documentation}
make\PYG{+w}{ }clean\PYG{+w}{     }\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Remove build artifacts}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Build ordering:} The Makefile handles workspace dependencies automatically.


\subsubsection{Package Management}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/architecture:package-management}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Workspace packages reference each other via \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{tool.uv.sources}}:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{k}{[}\PYG{k}{tool}\PYG{k}{.}\PYG{k}{uv}\PYG{k}{.}\PYG{k}{sources}\PYG{k}{]}
\PYG{n}{pycypher}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{p}{\PYGZob{}}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{n}{workspace}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{p}{=}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{k+kc}{true}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{p}{\PYGZcb{}}
\PYG{n}{shared}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{p}{\PYGZob{}}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{n}{workspace}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{p}{=}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{k+kc}{true}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{p}{\PYGZcb{}}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{After editing ``pyproject.toml``:} Always run \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{uv sync}}


\subsection{Performance Characteristics}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/architecture:performance-characteristics}}

\subsubsection{Parser}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/architecture:parser}}\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Grammar:} Earley parser (handles ambiguous grammars; trades speed for
expressiveness vs LALR)

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Caching:} Grammar compiled once per process (\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{get\_default\_parser()}}
singleton).  Parse result (AST) also cached by query string with LRU
capacity 512 — repeated queries are O(1) lookups.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Cold parse:} \textasciitilde{}56 ms per unique query; \sphinxstylestrong{warm (cached):} < 0.1 ms.

\end{itemize}


\subsubsection{Query Execution}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/architecture:query-execution}}\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Translation:} O(n) in AST size

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Property lookups:} ID\sphinxhyphen{}keyed, vectorised via pandas \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{set\_index}} +
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{map}}; never fetched unless referenced in a clause.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Aggregation:} Grouped aggregation uses pandas native Cython paths
(\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{"count"}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{"mean"}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{"min"}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{"max"}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{grouped.sum()}},
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{grouped.std()}}) rather than per\sphinxhyphen{}group Python callbacks.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Scalar functions:} Math functions (\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{abs}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{ceil}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{floor}},
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{sign}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{sqrt}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{cbrt}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{log}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{log2}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{log10}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{exp}},
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{pow}}, and all trig functions) use numpy C\sphinxhyphen{}level array operations via
a \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{\_make\_math1\_np}} / \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{\_make\_trig1\_np}} factory pattern — one numpy
call per Series regardless of row count.  String predicate functions
(\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{startsWith}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{endsWith}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{contains}}) use the pandas \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{.str}}
Cython accessor.  These replace the previous \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{pd.Series.apply()}}
paths (one Python call per row) and give \textasciitilde{}3–5× speedup on large frames.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Performance tips:} Push WHERE predicates early; use inline property
filters \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{\{prop: val\}}} on node patterns to reduce scan sizes; avoid
unbounded variable\sphinxhyphen{}length paths (\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{*}}) on large graphs.

\end{itemize}

\section{Architecture Decision Records}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/index:architecture-decision-records}}\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/index::doc}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
This directory records significant architectural decisions made in PyCypher.
Each ADR describes the context, decision, alternatives considered, and
consequences.

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Format:} ADRs follow the \sphinxhref{https://cognitect.com/blog/2011/11/15/documenting-architecture-decisions}{Michael Nygard template}
— Context, Decision, Alternatives, Consequences.

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Status values:} \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{Accepted}} (active), \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{Superseded}} (replaced by another
ADR), \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{Deprecated}} (no longer relevant).

\sphinxstepscope


\subsection{ADR\sphinxhyphen{}001: BindingFrame as Core Intermediate Representation}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/001-binding-frame-ir:adr-001-bindingframe-as-core-intermediate-representation}}\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/001-binding-frame-ir::doc}}\begin{quote}\begin{description}
\sphinxlineitem{Status}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Accepted

\sphinxlineitem{Date}
\sphinxAtStartPar
2025\sphinxhyphen{}01

\sphinxlineitem{Relates to}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{packages/pycypher/src/pycypher/binding\_frame.py}}

\end{description}\end{quote}


\subsubsection{Context}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/001-binding-frame-ir:context}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The original query execution engine used opaque relational algebra operators
with 32\sphinxhyphen{}character hex \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{HASH\_ID}} column names and \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{EntityType\_\_propertyname}}
prefixed columns.  Every operator had to thread a \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{variable\_map}} dictionary
to translate between Cypher variable names and internal column names.  This
was fragile, error\sphinxhyphen{}prone, and made debugging nearly impossible — a DataFrame
dump showed hex IDs rather than recognizable variable names.


\subsubsection{Decision}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/001-binding-frame-ir:decision}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Replace the legacy relational algebra with \sphinxstylestrong{BindingFrame}, a DataFrame\sphinxhyphen{}native
IR where column names \sphinxstyleemphasis{are} Cypher variable names.  Each row represents one
possible assignment of entity/relationship IDs to those variables.  Properties
are fetched on demand via \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{get\_property(var, prop)}} rather than being stored
in the frame.

\sphinxAtStartPar
Key operations:
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{EntityScan(entity\_type, var\_name)}} — produces a single\sphinxhyphen{}column frame

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{RelationshipScan(rel\_type, var\_name)}} — produces a three\sphinxhyphen{}column frame
(rel ID, source, target)

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{join(left\_col, right\_col)}} — inner join on shared structural key

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{filter(mask)}} — boolean mask filter

\end{itemize}


\subsubsection{Alternatives Considered}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/001-binding-frame-ir:alternatives-considered}}\begin{enumerate}
\sphinxsetlistlabels{\arabic}{enumi}{enumii}{}{.}%
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Keep legacy relational algebra} — Too fragile; \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{variable\_map}} threading
was the root cause of multiple production bugs.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Prefixed column names} (\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{Person\_\_name}}) — Still bleeds internal naming
conventions into operator logic and forces every operator to understand
the prefix format.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Hash\sphinxhyphen{}ID columns with external symbol table} — Marginally cleaner but
still requires lookup\sphinxhyphen{}table indirection for every property access.

\end{enumerate}


\subsubsection{Consequences}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/001-binding-frame-ir:consequences}}\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Eliminates three fragility sources: opaque HASH\_IDs, prefixed columns,
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{variable\_map}} threading.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Self\sphinxhyphen{}documenting: a DataFrame dump shows \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{p}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{r}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{q}} instead of
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{a7f3...}}.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
On\sphinxhyphen{}demand property fetch via ID\sphinxhyphen{}keyed vectorized lookup avoids fetching
unused properties.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Requires \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{GraphIndexManager}} for efficient property/adjacency lookups.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
All evaluators, the pattern matcher, and the mutation engine operate
directly on BindingFrame columns.

\end{itemize}

\sphinxstepscope


\subsection{ADR\sphinxhyphen{}002: Lark Parser with Earley Algorithm}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/002-lark-earley-parser:adr-002-lark-parser-with-earley-algorithm}}\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/002-lark-earley-parser::doc}}\begin{quote}\begin{description}
\sphinxlineitem{Status}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Accepted

\sphinxlineitem{Date}
\sphinxAtStartPar
2024

\sphinxlineitem{Relates to}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{packages/pycypher/src/pycypher/grammar\_parser.py}}

\end{description}\end{quote}


\subsubsection{Context}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/002-lark-earley-parser:context}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
PyCypher needs a complete Cypher grammar parser.  Cypher has complex operator
precedence, optional clauses, and syntactic ambiguities (e.g., \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{MATCH (n)}}
could be a function call or a node pattern depending on context).  The parser
must handle the full grammar without requiring manual disambiguation.


\subsubsection{Decision}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/002-lark-earley-parser:decision}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Use \sphinxstylestrong{Lark} with the \sphinxstylestrong{Earley} parsing algorithm.  Earley handles ambiguous
and context\sphinxhyphen{}sensitive grammars without requiring the grammar author to
restructure rules for lookahead constraints.

\sphinxAtStartPar
The compiled parser is cached as a process\sphinxhyphen{}level singleton via
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{get\_default\_parser()}}; application code must not construct \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{GrammarParser()}}
directly.  Parse results (ASTs) are also cached with LRU capacity of 512
entries.


\subsubsection{Alternatives Considered}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/002-lark-earley-parser:alternatives-considered}}\begin{enumerate}
\sphinxsetlistlabels{\arabic}{enumi}{enumii}{}{.}%
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{LALR parser} (Lark also supports LALR) — Faster, but requires the
grammar to be unambiguous.  Cypher’s syntax makes this difficult without
significant grammar restructuring.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{ANTLR} — Powerful but requires a Java runtime for grammar compilation
and generates code that is harder to integrate into a pure\sphinxhyphen{}Python project.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Hand\sphinxhyphen{}written recursive descent} — Maximum flexibility but extremely
labor\sphinxhyphen{}intensive for a grammar as large as Cypher, and difficult to maintain
as the grammar evolves.

\end{enumerate}


\subsubsection{Consequences}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/002-lark-earley-parser:consequences}}\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Cold parse: \textasciitilde{}56 ms per unique query; warm (cached): < 0.1 ms.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Grammar changes are expressed declaratively in \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{.lark}} files rather than
imperative parser code.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Earley’s O(n³) worst case is acceptable for query strings (typically < 1 KB).

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{CompositeTransformer}} converts raw parse trees to typed Pydantic AST nodes
via Lark’s visitor/transformer mechanism.

\end{itemize}

\sphinxstepscope


\subsection{ADR\sphinxhyphen{}003: Pydantic\sphinxhyphen{}Based Strongly\sphinxhyphen{}Typed AST Models}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/003-pydantic-ast-models:adr-003-pydantic-based-strongly-typed-ast-models}}\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/003-pydantic-ast-models::doc}}\begin{quote}\begin{description}
\sphinxlineitem{Status}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Accepted

\sphinxlineitem{Date}
\sphinxAtStartPar
2024

\sphinxlineitem{Relates to}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{packages/pycypher/src/pycypher/ast\_models.py}}

\end{description}\end{quote}


\subsubsection{Context}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/003-pydantic-ast-models:context}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
After Lark parses a Cypher query, the result is a raw parse tree of
dictionaries and lists.  Downstream consumers (semantic validation, query
planning, evaluators) need to safely navigate and pattern\sphinxhyphen{}match on AST nodes.
Dict\sphinxhyphen{}based trees are error\sphinxhyphen{}prone — a typo in a key name produces \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{None}}
rather than an error, and there is no IDE support for available fields.


\subsubsection{Decision}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/003-pydantic-ast-models:decision}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Define all AST node types as \sphinxstylestrong{Pydantic BaseModel} classes with explicit field
declarations and type annotations.  \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{ASTConverter}} translates the raw Lark
parse tree into typed Pydantic nodes.

\sphinxAtStartPar
Examples: \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{Query}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{Match}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{Return}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{NodePattern}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{Variable}},
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{PropertyAccess}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{FunctionCall}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{BinaryOp}}.


\subsubsection{Alternatives Considered}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/003-pydantic-ast-models:alternatives-considered}}\begin{enumerate}
\sphinxsetlistlabels{\arabic}{enumi}{enumii}{}{.}%
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Dict\sphinxhyphen{}based AST} — Standard in many parser projects but loses type safety,
IDE support, and validation.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Dataclasses} — Lighter weight but lacks built\sphinxhyphen{}in validation, JSON
serialization, and schema generation that Pydantic provides.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{TypedDict} — Provides type hints but no runtime validation; still
dict\sphinxhyphen{}based so no method dispatch.

\end{enumerate}


\subsubsection{Consequences}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/003-pydantic-ast-models:consequences}}\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
AST node shapes are validated at construction time — malformed trees fail
fast with clear error messages.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
IDE autocompletion and type checking work across the entire AST traversal
pipeline.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Variables are uniformly represented as \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{Variable}} instances (not strings),
eliminating a class of type confusion bugs.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Serialization support enables AST caching and inter\sphinxhyphen{}process communication.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Slight construction overhead vs raw dicts, negligible relative to parse time.

\end{itemize}

\sphinxstepscope


\subsection{ADR\sphinxhyphen{}004: Query\sphinxhyphen{}Scoped Shadow Write Atomicity}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/004-shadow-write-atomicity:adr-004-query-scoped-shadow-write-atomicity}}\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/004-shadow-write-atomicity::doc}}\begin{quote}\begin{description}
\sphinxlineitem{Status}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Accepted

\sphinxlineitem{Date}
\sphinxAtStartPar
2025

\sphinxlineitem{Relates to}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{packages/pycypher/src/pycypher/mutation\_engine.py}}

\end{description}\end{quote}


\subsubsection{Context}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/004-shadow-write-atomicity:context}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Cypher queries with SET, CREATE, or DELETE clauses mutate the in\sphinxhyphen{}memory graph.
If a query fails mid\sphinxhyphen{}execution (e.g., a type error in a later clause), the
context could be left in a partially\sphinxhyphen{}mutated state — some entities updated,
others not.  This violates the principle of least surprise and makes error
recovery impossible.


\subsubsection{Decision}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/004-shadow-write-atomicity:decision}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Buffer all mutations in a per\sphinxhyphen{}query \sphinxstylestrong{shadow layer} on \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{Context}}.  The
mutation lifecycle is:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{n}{context}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{begin\PYGZus{}query}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}    \PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} initialise shadow layer}
\PYG{k}{try}\PYG{p}{:}
    \PYG{n}{result} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{execute\PYGZus{}inner}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{query}\PYG{p}{)}
    \PYG{n}{context}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{commit\PYGZus{}query}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)}   \PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} promote shadows to canonical tables}
    \PYG{k}{return} \PYG{n}{result}
\PYG{k}{except} \PYG{n+ne}{Exception}\PYG{p}{:}
    \PYG{n}{context}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{rollback\PYGZus{}query}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{)} \PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} discard all pending mutations}
    \PYG{k}{raise}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{MutationEngine}} coordinates CREATE, SET, DELETE, REMOVE, and MERGE
operations, writing exclusively to the shadow layer until commit.


\subsubsection{Alternatives Considered}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/004-shadow-write-atomicity:alternatives-considered}}\begin{enumerate}
\sphinxsetlistlabels{\arabic}{enumi}{enumii}{}{.}%
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Direct mutation} — Simplest implementation but breaks atomicity; no
recovery from partial failures.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Per\sphinxhyphen{}clause checkpoints} — More granular but significantly more complex;
unclear what “partial success” means for a multi\sphinxhyphen{}clause query.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Undo log with replay} — Possible but adds complexity for a scenario
where discard\sphinxhyphen{}and\sphinxhyphen{}retry is simpler and sufficient.

\end{enumerate}


\subsubsection{Consequences}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/004-shadow-write-atomicity:consequences}}\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Failed queries never leave the context in a partially\sphinxhyphen{}mutated state.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Shadow layer memory is proportional to the number of mutated entities
(not total graph size).

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Query semantics are predictable and testable.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
MERGE operations can check existing state while buffering new writes.

\end{itemize}

\sphinxstepscope


\subsection{ADR\sphinxhyphen{}005: Composable Evaluator Pattern}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/005-composable-evaluators:adr-005-composable-evaluator-pattern}}\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/005-composable-evaluators::doc}}\begin{quote}\begin{description}
\sphinxlineitem{Status}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Accepted

\sphinxlineitem{Date}
\sphinxAtStartPar
2025

\sphinxlineitem{Relates to}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{packages/pycypher/src/pycypher/evaluator\_protocol.py}}

\end{description}\end{quote}


\subsubsection{Context}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/005-composable-evaluators:context}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Expression evaluation in Cypher spans arithmetic, boolean logic, string
predicates, comparisons, aggregation, collection operations, and function
calls.  A single monolithic evaluator class would be thousands of lines long
and create circular import dependencies between the expression evaluator
and the aggregation system.


\subsubsection{Decision}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/005-composable-evaluators:decision}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Decompose expression evaluation into focused, single\sphinxhyphen{}responsibility evaluator
classes that compose via delegation through an \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{ExpressionEvaluatorProtocol}}:
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{BindingExpressionEvaluator}} — main coordinator

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{ArithmeticEvaluator}} — \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{+}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{\sphinxhyphen{}}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{*}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{/}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{\%}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{\textasciicircum{}}}

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{BooleanEvaluator}} — \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{AND}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{OR}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{NOT}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{XOR}}

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{ComparisonEvaluator}} — \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{=}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{<>}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{<}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{>}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{<=}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{>=}}

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{ScalarFunctionEvaluator}} — built\sphinxhyphen{}in and user\sphinxhyphen{}defined functions

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{StringPredicateEvaluator}} — \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{STARTS WITH}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{ENDS WITH}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{CONTAINS}}

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{AggregationEvaluator}} — \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{COUNT}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{SUM}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{AVG}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{COLLECT}}, etc.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{CollectionEvaluator}} — list comprehensions, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{UNWIND}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{range()}}

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{ExistsEvaluator}} — \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{EXISTS \{ ... \}}} subqueries

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
The \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{ExpressionEvaluatorProtocol}} breaks circular imports by defining the
interface that evaluators depend on rather than concrete implementations.


\subsubsection{Alternatives Considered}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/005-composable-evaluators:alternatives-considered}}\begin{enumerate}
\sphinxsetlistlabels{\arabic}{enumi}{enumii}{}{.}%
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Monolithic evaluator} — Simpler dispatch but creates a multi\sphinxhyphen{}thousand\sphinxhyphen{}line
file with circular import issues.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Visitor pattern} with separate visitors per expression type — More
traditional but harder to compose and share evaluation context.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Expression compilation to bytecode} — Maximum performance but
significantly more complex; premature optimization for current workloads.

\end{enumerate}


\subsubsection{Consequences}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/005-composable-evaluators:consequences}}\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Each evaluator can be developed, tested, and optimized independently.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Vectorization is applied per\sphinxhyphen{}evaluator (numpy for math, pandas \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{.str}}
for strings).

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Circular import chains eliminated via protocol\sphinxhyphen{}based dependency inversion.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Slightly higher dispatch overhead, negligible vs actual computation cost.

\end{itemize}

\sphinxstepscope


\subsection{ADR\sphinxhyphen{}006: Pluggable Backend Engine Protocol}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/006-pluggable-backends:adr-006-pluggable-backend-engine-protocol}}\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/006-pluggable-backends::doc}}\begin{quote}\begin{description}
\sphinxlineitem{Status}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Accepted

\sphinxlineitem{Date}
\sphinxAtStartPar
2025

\sphinxlineitem{Relates to}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{packages/pycypher/src/pycypher/backend\_engine.py}}

\end{description}\end{quote}


\subsubsection{Context}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/006-pluggable-backends:context}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
PyCypher’s execution engine was tightly coupled to pandas.  For large datasets,
pandas becomes a bottleneck — it cannot push predicates into native C code
and struggles with datasets that exceed memory.  Users need the option to use
DuckDB (analytical SQL), Polars (Arrow\sphinxhyphen{}native lazy evaluation), or Dask
(distributed) without changing their Cypher queries.


\subsubsection{Decision}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/006-pluggable-backends:decision}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Define a \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{BackendEngine}} protocol that captures \textasciitilde{}15 primitive DataFrame
operations (filter, join, project, aggregate, sort, limit, etc.).  Three
implementations:
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{PandasBackend}} — default, zero\sphinxhyphen{}cost wrapper around existing pandas code

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{DuckDBBackend}} — pushes operations to DuckDB’s SQL engine for analytical
workloads

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{PolarsBackend}} — uses Polars lazy evaluation for Arrow\sphinxhyphen{}native execution

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
Selection via \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{Context(backend="duckdb")}} or \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{PYCYPHER\_BACKEND}} environment
variable.  The \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{"auto"}} mode selects the backend based on dataset size and
available libraries.


\subsubsection{Alternatives Considered}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/006-pluggable-backends:alternatives-considered}}\begin{enumerate}
\sphinxsetlistlabels{\arabic}{enumi}{enumii}{}{.}%
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Hard\sphinxhyphen{}code pandas throughout} — No migration path for large datasets.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Abstract all 918 pandas API calls} — Discovered during audit; impractical
to abstract every call.  The \textasciitilde{}15 primitive operations capture the essential
semantics.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Use an existing dataframe abstraction library} (e.g., Ibis, Narwhals) —
None were mature enough at decision time, and the abstraction surface was
small enough to own.

\end{enumerate}


\subsubsection{Consequences}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/006-pluggable-backends:consequences}}\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Users opt into DuckDB/Polars transparently — no query changes needed.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
User\sphinxhyphen{}facing API always returns \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{pd.DataFrame}} via \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{to\_pandas()}} escape
hatch for compatibility.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
All existing pandas\sphinxhyphen{}based code unaffected; backend adoption is incremental.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\textasciitilde{}15 operations is a manageable abstraction surface to maintain.

\end{itemize}

\sphinxstepscope


\subsection{ADR\sphinxhyphen{}007: Graph\sphinxhyphen{}Native Index Structures}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/007-graph-native-indexes:adr-007-graph-native-index-structures}}\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/007-graph-native-indexes::doc}}\begin{quote}\begin{description}
\sphinxlineitem{Status}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Accepted

\sphinxlineitem{Date}
\sphinxAtStartPar
2025

\sphinxlineitem{Relates to}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{packages/pycypher/src/pycypher/graph\_index.py}}

\end{description}\end{quote}


\subsubsection{Context}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/007-graph-native-indexes:context}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Pattern matching in Cypher requires finding neighbors, looking up entities by
property values, and filtering by labels.  Without indexes, every pattern
match performs a full table scan — O(E) for each relationship traversal and
O(N) for each property filter.  For graphs with millions of edges, this makes
interactive queries impractical.


\subsubsection{Decision}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/007-graph-native-indexes:decision}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Implement three graph\sphinxhyphen{}native index types managed by \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{GraphIndexManager}}:
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{AdjacencyIndex} — maps \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{(entity\_id, direction)}} to neighbor lists.
Reduces neighbor lookups from O(E) to O(degree).

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{PropertyValueIndex} — maps \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{(entity\_type, property, value)}} to ID sets
via hash table.  Reduces equality filters from O(N) to O(1).

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{EntityLabelIndex} — sorted ID arrays per label.  Reduces label membership
tests to O(log n) via binary search.

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
Indexes are built lazily on first access and invalidated on mutations (CREATE,
SET, DELETE).  Index data uses frozen tuples for thread\sphinxhyphen{}safe concurrent reads.


\subsubsection{Alternatives Considered}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/007-graph-native-indexes:alternatives-considered}}\begin{enumerate}
\sphinxsetlistlabels{\arabic}{enumi}{enumii}{}{.}%
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{No indexing} — Unacceptable for any non\sphinxhyphen{}trivial graph size.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{B\sphinxhyphen{}tree indexes} — More overhead for construction and maintenance; similar
lookup performance for equality queries, slightly better for range queries
which are uncommon in pattern matching.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Hash\sphinxhyphen{}only indexes} — Would work for equality but not ordered access;
the sorted label index enables efficient range scans and set intersection.

\end{enumerate}


\subsubsection{Consequences}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/007-graph-native-indexes:consequences}}\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Pattern matching goes from O(E) to O(degree) for each relationship traversal.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Enables the query planner to choose between hash join, broadcast join,
merge join, or nested\sphinxhyphen{}loop join based on index availability and estimated
cardinalities.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Memory cost proportional to data size (index storage mirrors data storage).

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Lazy construction avoids upfront cost for indexes that may never be needed.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Invalidation on mutation ensures index consistency without explicit
management.

\end{itemize}

\sphinxstepscope


\subsection{ADR\sphinxhyphen{}008: Monorepo Workspace Structure}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/008-monorepo-workspace:adr-008-monorepo-workspace-structure}}\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/008-monorepo-workspace::doc}}\begin{quote}\begin{description}
\sphinxlineitem{Status}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Accepted

\sphinxlineitem{Date}
\sphinxAtStartPar
2024

\sphinxlineitem{Relates to}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{pyproject.toml}} (root workspace configuration)

\end{description}\end{quote}


\subsubsection{Context}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/008-monorepo-workspace:context}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The project contains three logical components: shared utilities, the Cypher
query engine, and an ETL pipeline for open data.  These share common logging,
configuration, and helper code.  They need to be developed, tested, and (in
some cases) released independently.


\subsubsection{Decision}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/008-monorepo-workspace:decision}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Use a \sphinxstylestrong{uv workspace monorepo} with three packages:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
packages/
├── shared/         \PYGZsh{} Common utilities, logging (no internal deps)
├── pycypher/       \PYGZsh{} Cypher parser and query engine (depends on shared)
└── fastopendata/   \PYGZsh{} ETL pipeline (depends on shared + pycypher)
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
Cross\sphinxhyphen{}package dependencies are declared in \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{tool.uv.sources}} as workspace
references.  Dependency groups (\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{dev\sphinxhyphen{}core}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{dev}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{dev\sphinxhyphen{}full}}) provide
layered developer environments from lightweight to comprehensive.


\subsubsection{Alternatives Considered}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/008-monorepo-workspace:alternatives-considered}}\begin{enumerate}
\sphinxsetlistlabels{\arabic}{enumi}{enumii}{}{.}%
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Separate git repositories} with pip/uv dependencies — Adds friction to
cross\sphinxhyphen{}package changes (separate PRs, version pinning, publish cycles).

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Single monolithic package} — Simpler packaging but forces all users to
install ETL dependencies even if they only need the query engine.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Git submodules} — Historically fragile; adds clone complexity and makes
atomic cross\sphinxhyphen{}package commits difficult.

\end{enumerate}


\subsubsection{Consequences}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/008-monorepo-workspace:consequences}}\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Cross\sphinxhyphen{}package changes are atomic (single commit, single PR).

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Dependency order (\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{shared}} → \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{pycypher}} → \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{fastopendata}}) prevents
circular imports at the package level.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{uv sync}} must be run after \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{pyproject.toml}} changes — CI enforces this
with a \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{lock\sphinxhyphen{}check}} job.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Users can install \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{pycypher}} alone without \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{fastopendata}} dependencies.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
All tests live in a single \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{tests/}} directory for unified test execution.

\end{itemize}

\sphinxstepscope


\subsection{ADR\sphinxhyphen{}009: Star as Central Query Coordinator}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/009-star-coordinator:adr-009-star-as-central-query-coordinator}}\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/009-star-coordinator::doc}}\begin{quote}\begin{description}
\sphinxlineitem{Status}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Accepted (with known limitations)

\sphinxlineitem{Date}
\sphinxAtStartPar
2024

\sphinxlineitem{Relates to}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{packages/pycypher/src/pycypher/star.py}}

\end{description}\end{quote}


\subsubsection{Context}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/009-star-coordinator:context}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Users need a single entry point to execute Cypher queries against an in\sphinxhyphen{}memory
graph.  The execution pipeline involves parsing, AST conversion, semantic
validation, query planning, clause\sphinxhyphen{}by\sphinxhyphen{}clause execution, mutation coordination,
and result formatting.  These steps must be orchestrated in the correct order
with proper error handling and resource management.


\subsubsection{Decision}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/009-star-coordinator:decision}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Implement \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{Star}} as the main user\sphinxhyphen{}facing coordinator.  The primary API is:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{n}{star} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{Star}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{context}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{n}{context}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{n}{result} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{star}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{execute\PYGZus{}query}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{MATCH (p:Person) RETURN p.name}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{Star}} delegates to focused components:
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{get\_default\_parser()}} for parsing

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{ASTConverter}} for AST conversion

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{SemanticValidator}} for pre\sphinxhyphen{}execution validation

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{PatternMatcher}} for MATCH clause execution

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{BindingExpressionEvaluator}} for expression evaluation

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{MutationEngine}} for CREATE/SET/DELETE

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{QueryProfiler}} for timing (optional)

\end{itemize}


\subsubsection{Alternatives Considered}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/009-star-coordinator:alternatives-considered}}\begin{enumerate}
\sphinxsetlistlabels{\arabic}{enumi}{enumii}{}{.}%
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Separate parser, planner, executor classes with factory} — More modular
but adds ceremony for the common case.  Users would need to assemble a
pipeline before executing a query.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Functional pipeline} (compose functions) — Clean but makes shared state
(context, profiler, cache) awkward to thread through.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Builder pattern} — \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{QueryBuilder().parse(q).validate().plan().execute()}}
— Flexible but verbose for the 90\% case of “just run this query.”

\end{enumerate}


\subsubsection{Consequences}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/009-star-coordinator:consequences}}\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Simple, discoverable API: \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{Star.execute\_query()}} does everything.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{Star}} is becoming a god object — it coordinates too many concerns
(see Task \#6: “Decompose Star class”).  Future work should extract a
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{QueryExecutor}} that owns the pipeline while \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{Star}} remains a
thin user\sphinxhyphen{}facing facade.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Adding new pipeline stages (e.g., cost\sphinxhyphen{}based planning) requires modifying
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{Star}}, which is not ideal for extensibility.

\end{itemize}

\sphinxstepscope


\subsection{ADR\sphinxhyphen{}010: Vectorized Scalar Function Evaluation}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/010-vectorized-evaluation:adr-010-vectorized-scalar-function-evaluation}}\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/010-vectorized-evaluation::doc}}\begin{quote}\begin{description}
\sphinxlineitem{Status}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Accepted

\sphinxlineitem{Date}
\sphinxAtStartPar
2025

\sphinxlineitem{Relates to}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{packages/pycypher/src/pycypher/scalar\_function\_evaluator.py}}

\end{description}\end{quote}


\subsubsection{Context}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/010-vectorized-evaluation:context}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Scalar functions (math, string, type predicates) were initially implemented
using \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{pd.Series.apply(func)}} which calls a Python function once per row.
For large DataFrames (100K+ rows), this is 10–100x slower than vectorized
alternatives because of Python function\sphinxhyphen{}call overhead per row.


\subsubsection{Decision}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/010-vectorized-evaluation:decision}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Replace \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{pd.Series.apply()}} with vectorized operations wherever possible:
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Math functions} (\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{abs}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{ceil}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{floor}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{sqrt}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{log}}, trig
functions, etc.) use numpy C\sphinxhyphen{}level array operations via
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{\_make\_math1\_np()}} / \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{\_make\_trig1\_np()}} factory functions — one numpy
call per Series regardless of row count.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{String predicates} (\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{STARTS WITH}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{ENDS WITH}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{CONTAINS}}) use
the pandas \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{.str}} Cython accessor (\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{.str.startswith()}},
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{.str.endswith()}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{.str.contains()}}).

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Aggregations} (\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{COUNT}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{SUM}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{AVG}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{MIN}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{MAX}}) use
pandas native grouped Cython paths rather than per\sphinxhyphen{}group Python callbacks.

\end{itemize}


\subsubsection{Alternatives Considered}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/010-vectorized-evaluation:alternatives-considered}}\begin{enumerate}
\sphinxsetlistlabels{\arabic}{enumi}{enumii}{}{.}%
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Keep ``pd.Series.apply()``} — Simplest but unacceptable performance
on large datasets.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Numba JIT compilation} — Maximum performance for numeric operations
but adds a heavy dependency and compilation latency on first call.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Compile expressions to SQL for DuckDB execution} — Attractive for the
DuckDB backend but does not help the pandas\sphinxhyphen{}only path which is the default.

\end{enumerate}


\subsubsection{Consequences}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/010-vectorized-evaluation:consequences}}\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
3–5x speedup on large frames for math and string operations.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Null handling requires explicit pre\sphinxhyphen{}allocation and masking (numpy operations
do not propagate pandas NA natively).

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Math functions must be stateless to be vectorizable.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Tight coupling to numpy/pandas internals — tested against pinned versions
to prevent breakage on library updates.

\end{itemize}

\sphinxstepscope


\subsection{ADR\sphinxhyphen{}011: Grammar Rule Mixins Package Split}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/011-grammar-mixin-package:adr-011-grammar-rule-mixins-package-split}}\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/011-grammar-mixin-package::doc}}\begin{quote}\begin{description}
\sphinxlineitem{Status}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Accepted

\sphinxlineitem{Date}
\sphinxAtStartPar
2026\sphinxhyphen{}03

\sphinxlineitem{Relates to}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{packages/pycypher/src/pycypher/grammar\_rule\_mixins/}}

\sphinxlineitem{Supersedes}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Original monolithic \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{grammar\_rule\_mixins.py}}

\end{description}\end{quote}


\subsubsection{Context}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/011-grammar-mixin-package:context}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The original \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{grammar\_rule\_mixins.py}} was a single file containing all Lark
transformer methods for converting raw parse trees into Pydantic AST nodes.
As the Cypher grammar grew, this file exceeded 3,000 lines covering five
unrelated domains (literals, expressions, patterns, functions, clauses).
This made the file difficult to navigate, review, and test in isolation.

\sphinxAtStartPar
Additionally, during concurrent AST cache warmup, per\sphinxhyphen{}call Rich logging
in the monolithic transformer caused \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{OSError: bad file descriptor}} errors
due to file descriptor contention.


\subsubsection{Decision}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/011-grammar-mixin-package:decision}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Split the monolithic file into a \sphinxstylestrong{Python package} with five focused mixin
modules:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
grammar\PYGZus{}rule\PYGZus{}mixins/
├── \PYGZus{}\PYGZus{}init\PYGZus{}\PYGZus{}.py       \PYGZsh{} Re\PYGZhy{}exports all 5 mixins
├── literals.py       \PYGZsh{} LiteralRulesMixin (278 lines)
├── expressions.py    \PYGZsh{} ExpressionRulesMixin (683 lines)
├── patterns.py       \PYGZsh{} PatternRulesMixin (520 lines)
├── functions.py      \PYGZsh{} FunctionRulesMixin (594 lines)
└── clauses.py        \PYGZsh{} ClauseRulesMixin (1,165 lines)
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{CompositeTransformer}} in \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{grammar\_transformers.py}} delegates to
specialized transformer classes (\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{LiteralTransformer}},
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{ExpressionTransformer}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{PatternTransformer}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{StatementTransformer}})
that inherit from these mixins.  Method resolution is cached on the instance
after first lookup to avoid repeated MRO iteration during concurrent warmup.


\subsubsection{Alternatives Considered}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/011-grammar-mixin-package:alternatives-considered}}\begin{enumerate}
\sphinxsetlistlabels{\arabic}{enumi}{enumii}{}{.}%
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Keep monolithic file} — Too large for effective review and navigation;
concurrent Rich logging bug made this untenable.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Separate transformer classes per rule group} — Would break Lark’s
method\sphinxhyphen{}name\sphinxhyphen{}based dispatch, which expects all visitor methods on a single
transformer instance.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Decorator\sphinxhyphen{}based organization} — Methods stay in one file but are tagged
by domain.  Doesn’t reduce file size or enable independent testing.

\end{enumerate}


\subsubsection{Consequences}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/011-grammar-mixin-package:consequences}}\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Each mixin can be understood, reviewed, and tested independently.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Lark’s visitor mechanism is preserved via Python MRO (multiple inheritance).

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{\_\_init\_\_.py}} re\sphinxhyphen{}exports all mixins, so \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{from pycypher.grammar\_rule\_mixins
import ClauseRulesMixin}} continues to work.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Method\sphinxhyphen{}resolution caching eliminates the Rich \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{OSError}} during concurrent
AST cache warmup.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Import path changed from module to package — existing \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{from
pycypher.grammar\_rule\_mixins import ...}} imports are unaffected.

\end{itemize}

\sphinxstepscope


\subsection{ADR\sphinxhyphen{}012: Automatic Test Markers and Isolation Fixtures}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/012-auto-test-markers:adr-012-automatic-test-markers-and-isolation-fixtures}}\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/012-auto-test-markers::doc}}\begin{quote}\begin{description}
\sphinxlineitem{Status}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Accepted

\sphinxlineitem{Date}
\sphinxAtStartPar
2026\sphinxhyphen{}03

\sphinxlineitem{Relates to}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{tests/conftest.py}}

\end{description}\end{quote}


\subsubsection{Context}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/012-auto-test-markers:context}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
With \textasciitilde{}300 test files across multiple directories, manually applying pytest
markers (\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{@pytest.mark.integration}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{@pytest.mark.performance}}, etc.)
was inconsistent — many tests lacked markers, making it difficult to run
targeted subsets (e.g., \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{\sphinxhyphen{}m "not slow"}}).  Additionally, two classes of
cross\sphinxhyphen{}test contamination caused flaky failures:
\begin{enumerate}
\sphinxsetlistlabels{\arabic}{enumi}{enumii}{}{.}%
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{SIGALRM leakage} — Tests using \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{timeout\_seconds}} set a POSIX alarm
that could fire during a subsequent, unrelated test, causing spurious
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{QueryTimeoutError}}.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Logger level contamination} — Tests using \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{caplog.at\_level(DEBUG)}}
left \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{shared.logger.LOGGER}} in DEBUG mode, causing subsequent tests
to emit unexpected log output.

\end{enumerate}


\subsubsection{Decision}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/012-auto-test-markers:decision}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Add automatic marker inference and isolation fixtures to \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{conftest.py}}:

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Auto\sphinxhyphen{}markers} (applied when no explicit marker is present):
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{tests/benchmarks/}} → \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{performance}}

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{tests/large\_dataset/}} → \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{integration}} + \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{slow}}

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{tests/load\_testing/}} → \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{performance}} + \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{slow}}

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{tests/property\_based/}} → \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{unit}}

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Filenames containing \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{\_e2e\_}} or \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{\_end\_to\_end}} → \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{integration}}

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Filenames containing \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{\_performance\_}} → \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{performance}}

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Filenames containing \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{\_security\_}} → \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{security}}

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Per\sphinxhyphen{}marker timeouts:}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{integration}}: 120s (vs 30s default)

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{slow}}: 300s (vs 30s default)

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Isolation fixtures} (auto\sphinxhyphen{}use, session\sphinxhyphen{}wide):
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{\_clear\_pending\_sigalrm()}} — Resets SIGALRM after each test.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{\_restore\_shared\_logger\_level()}} — Saves and restores logger level.

\end{itemize}


\subsubsection{Alternatives Considered}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/012-auto-test-markers:alternatives-considered}}\begin{enumerate}
\sphinxsetlistlabels{\arabic}{enumi}{enumii}{}{.}%
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Manual markers everywhere} — Reliable but requires discipline; 300+
files means markers would drift out of date.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{pytest plugin with custom collection hook} — More powerful but adds
external dependency or maintenance burden for a custom plugin.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Directory\sphinxhyphen{}based conftest.py files} — Each subdirectory gets its own
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{conftest.py}} that applies markers.  Works but scatters configuration
across many files.

\end{enumerate}


\subsubsection{Consequences}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/adr/012-auto-test-markers:consequences}}\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Running \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{pytest \sphinxhyphen{}m "not slow"}} now reliably excludes all slow tests,
even those that were never manually marked.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Explicit \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{@pytest.mark.X}} always takes precedence — auto\sphinxhyphen{}markers never
override developer intent.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
SIGALRM and logger contamination eliminated, reducing flaky test rate.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{perf\_threshold()}} helper scales timing assertions for CI environments
(configurable via \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{PYCYPHER\_PERF\_MULTIPLIER}}).

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
New test files automatically get appropriate markers based on their
location — no action needed from the developer.

\end{itemize}

\sphinxstepscope
