\subsection{Overview}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/threat_model:overview}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
PyCypher\sphinxhyphen{}nmetl is an ETL pipeline system that:
\begin{enumerate}
\sphinxsetlistlabels{\arabic}{enumi}{enumii}{}{.}%
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Parses user\sphinxhyphen{}supplied Cypher queries against in\sphinxhyphen{}process data.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Ingests data from external sources (files, databases, cloud storage).

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Transforms data through a query execution engine.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Outputs results to files or graph databases (Neo4j).

\end{enumerate}

\sphinxAtStartPar
The system runs as a single\sphinxhyphen{}process application with no built\sphinxhyphen{}in
authentication or multi\sphinxhyphen{}tenancy. Security controls focus on input
validation, resource limits, and preventing unintended data access.


\subsection{Trust Boundaries}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/threat_model:trust-boundaries}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The system has five trust boundaries where data crosses from a
less\sphinxhyphen{}trusted domain into a more\sphinxhyphen{}trusted one.

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    UNTRUSTED ZONE                               │
│  User Cypher queries, YAML configs, file paths, SQL fragments  │
│  Environment variables, CLI arguments, external URIs           │
└──────────────┬──────────────────────────────────┬──────────────┘
               │ TB\PYGZhy{}1: User Input                 │ TB\PYGZhy{}2: Config
               ▼                                  ▼
┌──────────────────────┐        ┌─────────────────────────────┐
│  Query Parser \PYGZam{}      │        │  Config Loader              │
│  Semantic Validator   │        │  (YAML + env var subst.)    │
│  input\PYGZus{}validator.py   │        │  ingestion/config.py        │
└──────────┬───────────┘        └──────────┬──────────────────┘
           │                               │
           ▼                               ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              VALIDATED ZONE (in\PYGZhy{}process)                      │
│  Parsed AST, validated config, sanitized identifiers          │
└───────┬──────────────────┬──────────────────┬────────────────┘
        │ TB\PYGZhy{}3: Data I/O   │ TB\PYGZhy{}4: SQL Exec   │ TB\PYGZhy{}5: Output
        ▼                  ▼                   ▼
┌──────────────┐  ┌───────────────┐  ┌─────────────────────┐
│ File System  │  │ DuckDB Engine │  │ Neo4j / File Output │
│ Cloud (S3/GS)│  │ (in\PYGZhy{}process)  │  │ (external)          │
└──────────────┘  └───────────────┘  └─────────────────────┘
\end{sphinxVerbatim}


\subsubsection{TB\sphinxhyphen{}1: User Input Boundary}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/threat_model:tb-1-user-input-boundary}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Crossing point:} User\sphinxhyphen{}supplied Cypher queries enter the system.

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Entry points:}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{star.execute\_query()}} — primary query API

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{star.add\_cypher\_query()}} — pipeline query registration

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
CLI \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{pycypher run}} command

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Protections:}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Cypher parser rejects malformed syntax
(\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{pycypher/parser.py}})

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Semantic validator detects undefined variables, type mismatches
(\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{pycypher/semantic\_validator.py}})

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Input validator enforces query ID uniqueness and non\sphinxhyphen{}empty content
(\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{pycypher/input\_validator.py:44\sphinxhyphen{}141}})

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Resource limits: cross\sphinxhyphen{}join row ceiling, query timeout, memory budget

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Residual risks:}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Valid but expensive queries (e.g., deep recursive traversals) may
still consume significant resources within configured limits.

\end{itemize}


\subsubsection{TB\sphinxhyphen{}2: Configuration Boundary}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/threat_model:tb-2-configuration-boundary}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Crossing point:} YAML pipeline configs and environment variables.

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Entry points:}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{load\_pipeline\_config()}} — YAML config loading
(\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{ingestion/config.py:829\sphinxhyphen{}854}})

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Environment variable substitution \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{\$\{VAR\}}} in config values
(\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{ingestion/config.py:743\sphinxhyphen{}826}})

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Protections:}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{yaml.safe\_load()}} prevents deserialization attacks
(\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{ingestion/config.py:852}})

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Blocked environment variables: \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{AWS\_SECRET\_ACCESS\_KEY}},
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{AWS\_SESSION\_TOKEN}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{AZURE\_CLIENT\_SECRET}},
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{GOOGLE\_APPLICATION\_CREDENTIALS}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{GCP\_SERVICE\_ACCOUNT\_KEY}},
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{API\_SECRET}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{SECRET\_KEY}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{PRIVATE\_KEY}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{GITHUB\_TOKEN}},
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{GITLAB\_TOKEN}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{NPM\_TOKEN}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{PYPI\_TOKEN}},
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{SSH\_PRIVATE\_KEY}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{SSL\_KEY}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{TLS\_KEY}}
(\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{ingestion/config.py:745\sphinxhyphen{}771}})

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Blocked prefixes: \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{PRIVATE\_}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{CREDENTIAL\_}}

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Blocked module imports in config context: \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{os}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{subprocess}},
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{pickle}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{ctypes}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{socket}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{shutil}}, etc.
(\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{ingestion/config.py:379\sphinxhyphen{}400}})

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Unresolved or blocked vars left as \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{\$\{VAR\}}} placeholders (fail\sphinxhyphen{}safe)

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Residual risks:}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Non\sphinxhyphen{}blocked env vars containing secrets (custom naming conventions)
will be substituted. Operators should audit env var names used in
configs.

\end{itemize}


\subsubsection{TB\sphinxhyphen{}3: Data I/O Boundary}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/threat_model:tb-3-data-i-o-boundary}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Crossing point:} Reading external data from files, databases, cloud.

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Entry points:}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{FileDataSource.read()}} — file\sphinxhyphen{}based data ingestion
(\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{ingestion/data\_sources.py:432}})

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
URI scheme handler: \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{file://}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{s3://}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{gs://}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{https://}},
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{postgresql://}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{mysql://}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{sqlite://}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{duckdb://}}, bare paths

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Protections:}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Path traversal prevention:}
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{sanitize\_file\_path()}} rejects \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{..}} components, blocks sensitive
directories (\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{/etc/}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{/root/}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{/proc/}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{/sys/}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{/dev/}},
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{/var/run/}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{/boot/}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{/sbin/}}), resolves symlinks to prevent
TOCTOU attacks (\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{ingestion/security.py:120\sphinxhyphen{}189}})

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{URI scheme allowlist:}
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{validate\_uri\_scheme()}} restricts to known\sphinxhyphen{}safe schemes
(\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{ingestion/security.py:560\sphinxhyphen{}609}})

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{SSRF protection:}
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{\_check\_ssrf\_hostname()}} blocks RFC 1918 private ranges, loopback,
link\sphinxhyphen{}local, reserved addresses, and internal hostnames
(\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{ingestion/security.py:612\sphinxhyphen{}697}}). DNS resolution failures are
rejected (fail\sphinxhyphen{}closed).

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Credential detection:}
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{mask\_uri\_credentials()}} masks passwords in URIs before logging
(\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{ingestion/security.py:770}}). CLI \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{security\_check}} command warns
on embedded credentials (\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{cli/security.py:120\sphinxhyphen{}143}}).

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Residual risks:}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Cloud storage access relies on ambient credentials (IAM roles, env
vars). Misconfigured IAM policies could expose unintended data.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
TOCTOU window between \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{resolve()}} and actual open is minimal but
non\sphinxhyphen{}zero on adversarial filesystems.

\end{itemize}


\subsubsection{TB\sphinxhyphen{}4: SQL Execution Boundary}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/threat_model:tb-4-sql-execution-boundary}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Crossing point:} SQL fragments passed to DuckDB for data reading.

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Entry points:}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{validate\_sql\_query()}} — SQL validation gate
(\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{ingestion/security.py:402\sphinxhyphen{}481}})

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{sanitize\_sql\_identifier()}} — table/column name validation
(\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{ingestion/security.py:484}})

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{validate\_identifier()}} — DuckDB backend identifier validation
(\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{backends/\_helpers.py:10\sphinxhyphen{}39}})

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Protections:}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Statement stacking prevention:} rejects multiple semicolons
(\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{ingestion/security.py:441}})

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{DDL/DML blocking:} rejects \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{DROP}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{ALTER}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{CREATE}},
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{TRUNCATE}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{INSERT}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{UPDATE}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{DELETE}}

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Dangerous function blocking:} \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{read\_csv}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{read\_parquet}},
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{read\_json}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{glob}}, and DuckDB system functions blocked
(\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{ingestion/security.py:317\sphinxhyphen{}341}})

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Dangerous prefix blocking:} \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{duckdb\_}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{pg\_}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{pragma\_}},
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{information\_schema}} (\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{ingestion/security.py:344\sphinxhyphen{}349}})

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Comment stripping:} removes \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{\sphinxhyphen{}\sphinxhyphen{}}} and \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{/* */}} before validation
(\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{ingestion/security.py:192}})

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{NUL byte rejection:} prevents C\sphinxhyphen{}level string truncation attacks
(\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{ingestion/security.py:503, 715}})

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Identifier pattern:} \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{\textasciicircum{}{[}A\sphinxhyphen{}Za\sphinxhyphen{}z\_{]}{[}A\sphinxhyphen{}Za\sphinxhyphen{}z0\sphinxhyphen{}9\_{]}*\$}} enforced

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Residual risks:}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
DuckDB runs in\sphinxhyphen{}process with full host permissions. A bypass of SQL
validation could access arbitrary host resources.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
New DuckDB versions may introduce functions not yet on the blocklist.

\end{itemize}


\subsubsection{TB\sphinxhyphen{}5: Output Boundary}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/threat_model:tb-5-output-boundary}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Crossing point:} Query results written to files or external databases.

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Entry points:}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{write\_dataframe\_to\_uri()}} — file output (CSV, Parquet, JSON)
(\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{ingestion/output\_writer.py:37\sphinxhyphen{}119}})

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{Neo4jSink}} — graph database output
(\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{sinks/neo4j.py:1\sphinxhyphen{}349}})

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Protections:}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{File output:} path validated via \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{sanitize\_file\_path()}}
(\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{output\_writer.py:86}})

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Neo4j Cypher injection prevention:}
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{\_validate\_cypher\_identifier()}} rejects backticks, NUL bytes,
curly braces, square brackets, backslashes
(\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{sinks/neo4j.py:241\sphinxhyphen{}298}}). Unicode normalization check for
lookalike character attacks (\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{neo4j.py:277}}). All identifiers
are backtick\sphinxhyphen{}quoted. Parameterized queries used for data values.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Internal column filtering:} \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{\_\_ID\_\_}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{\_\_SOURCE\_\_}},
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{\_\_TARGET\_\_}} automatically excluded from results via
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{properties()}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{keys()}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{RETURN *}}.

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Residual risks:}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Neo4j connection credentials stored in config or env vars. See
TB\sphinxhyphen{}2 for credential handling.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Output file permissions inherit from process umask.

\end{itemize}

\subsection{Audit \& Logging Architecture}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/threat_model:audit-logging-architecture}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Security audit log} (\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{pycypher.audit}}):
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Opt\sphinxhyphen{}in via \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{PYCYPHER\_AUDIT\_LOG}} environment variable

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Structured JSON output for SIEM integration

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Never logs parameter values — only parameter names

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Query text truncated to 2048 characters

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Separate logger from application logs

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Security event log} (\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{pycypher.security}}):
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Opt\sphinxhyphen{}in via \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{PYCYPHER\_SECURITY\_LOG}} environment variable

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Logs: event type, timestamp, truncated input (256 chars), source
function, detail

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Intended for incident response correlation

\end{itemize}


\subsection{Data Flow Summary}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/threat_model:data-flow-summary}}
\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
User Query ──► Parser ──► Semantic Validator ──► Query Planner
     │                                               │
     ▼                                               ▼
YAML Config ──► safe\PYGZus{}load ──► env subst ──► Pipeline Config
     │                                          │
     ▼                                          ▼
File/DB URI ──► scheme check ──► SSRF check ──► path sanitize
     │                                          │
     ▼                                          ▼
SQL fragment ──► comment strip ──► blocklist ──► DuckDB exec
     │                                          │
     ▼                                          ▼
Results ──► internal col filter ──► output path validate ──► Write
                                        │
                                        ▼
                                 Neo4j ──► identifier validate
                                        ──► parameterized query
\end{sphinxVerbatim}


\subsection{Coordination Requirements}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/threat_model:coordination-requirements}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Per Amendment Cycle 2, changes to the following require security
specialist coordination before merge:
\begin{enumerate}
\sphinxsetlistlabels{\arabic}{enumi}{enumii}{}{.}%
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Data entry/exit points} — any new data source type, output
format, or URI scheme.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{File I/O operations} — changes to file reading/writing paths
or permission handling.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Config parsing} — changes to YAML loading, env var substitution,
or module import blocking.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Credential handling} — changes to credential detection, masking,
or storage.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Input validation} — changes to SQL validation, path sanitization,
SSRF checking, or identifier validation.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Network operations} — changes to health server, SSRF protections,
or any new network endpoints.

\end{enumerate}


\subsection{Security Testing}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/threat_model:security-testing}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Existing security test coverage:
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{tests/test\_security\_sql\_injection\_tdd.py}} — SQL injection TDD

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{tests/test\_security\_sql\_injection\_proof.py}} — SQL injection proofs

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{tests/test\_backend\_engine\_security.py}} — backend engine security

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{tests/test\_data\_source\_security.py}} — data source URI validation

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{tests/test\_duckdb\_reader\_security.py}} — DuckDB reader security

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{tests/test\_helpers\_serialization\_security.py}} — serialization safety

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{tests/test\_neo4j\_sink\_security.py}} — Neo4j sink security

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{tests/test\_dask\_distributed\_security.py}} — distributed security

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{tests/test\_distributed\_security\_contracts.py}} — distributed contracts

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
Run all security tests:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
uv\PYG{+w}{ }run\PYG{+w}{ }pytest\PYG{+w}{ }tests/\PYG{+w}{ }\PYGZhy{}k\PYG{+w}{ }security\PYG{+w}{ }\PYGZhy{}q
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\section{New Contributors}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/index:new-contributors}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Start with the \sphinxhref{https://github.com/pycypher/pycypher-nmetl/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md}{CONTRIBUTING.md}
file at the repository root — it covers quick start setup, what to work on,
debugging tips, and the submission process.  The pages below go deeper into
each topic.


\section{Overview}
\label{\detokenize{developer_guide/index:overview}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
This guide is for developers who want to contribute to PyCypher:
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
{\hyperref[\detokenize{developer_guide/architecture::doc}]{\sphinxcrossref{\DUrole{doc}{Architecture}}}} — Monorepo layout, package dependencies, the
Lark\sphinxhyphen{}to\sphinxhyphen{}Pydantic parsing pipeline, and the BindingFrame execution model.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
{\hyperref[\detokenize{developer_guide/contributing::doc}]{\sphinxcrossref{\DUrole{doc}{Contributing}}}} — Development environment setup (\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{uv}}, Python 3.14t),
code style (ruff, ty, Google docstrings), branch naming, and PR process.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
{\hyperref[\detokenize{developer_guide/testing::doc}]{\sphinxcrossref{\DUrole{doc}{Testing}}}} — Running tests with pytest, writing new tests, parallel
execution, and coverage reporting.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
{\hyperref[\detokenize{developer_guide/release::doc}]{\sphinxcrossref{\DUrole{doc}{Release Process}}}} — Semantic versioning, pre\sphinxhyphen{}release checklist, building and
publishing packages, and CI/CD integration.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
{\hyperref[\detokenize{developer_guide/security::doc}]{\sphinxcrossref{\DUrole{doc}{Security Hardening \& Compliance}}}} — Threat model, input validation, resource limits,
audit logging, and deployment checklist.

\end{itemize}

\sphinxstepscope


\chapter{Architecture Decision Records}
\label{\detokenize{adr/index:architecture-decision-records}}\label{\detokenize{adr/index::doc}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
This directory documents significant architectural decisions made in PyCypher.
Each ADR captures the context, decision, and consequences of a design choice
so that future contributors understand \sphinxstyleemphasis{why} things are the way they are.

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Format:} Each ADR follows a consistent structure — Status, Context, Decision,
Consequences.  ADRs are numbered sequentially and never deleted; superseded
decisions are marked as such.

\sphinxstepscope


\section{ADR\sphinxhyphen{}001: Use Lark with Earley Algorithm for Cypher Parsing}
\label{\detokenize{adr/adr-001-lark-earley-parser:adr-001-use-lark-with-earley-algorithm-for-cypher-parsing}}\label{\detokenize{adr/adr-001-lark-earley-parser::doc}}\begin{quote}\begin{description}
\sphinxlineitem{Status}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Accepted

\sphinxlineitem{Date}
\sphinxAtStartPar
2024\sphinxhyphen{}01

\sphinxlineitem{Affects}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{packages/pycypher/src/pycypher/grammar\_parser.py}},
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{packages/pycypher/src/pycypher/grammar\_transformers.py}}

\end{description}\end{quote}


\subsection{Context}
\label{\detokenize{adr/adr-001-lark-earley-parser:context}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
PyCypher needs a parser that can handle the full openCypher grammar specification.
The grammar is complex, with ambiguous constructs (e.g. label predicates in WHERE
clauses, list comprehensions vs pattern comprehensions) that require a parser
capable of handling ambiguity.

\sphinxAtStartPar
Key requirements:
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Full openCypher BNF coverage

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Good error messages for malformed queries

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Reasonable cold\sphinxhyphen{}start performance for interactive use

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Maintainable grammar definition (not hand\sphinxhyphen{}written parser tables)

\end{itemize}


\subsection{Decision}
\label{\detokenize{adr/adr-001-lark-earley-parser:decision}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Use \sphinxhref{https://github.com/lark-parser/lark}{Lark} with the \sphinxstylestrong{Earley} parsing
algorithm.  The grammar is defined declaratively in Lark’s EBNF notation,
derived from the official openCypher BNF specification.

\sphinxAtStartPar
To eliminate cold\sphinxhyphen{}start latency (\textasciitilde{}96ms for grammar compilation), the parser
is compiled eagerly in a daemon thread at module import time and cached as a
process\sphinxhyphen{}level singleton via \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{get\_default\_parser()}}.

\sphinxAtStartPar
The Lark parse tree is transformed into Pydantic AST models through a
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{CompositeTransformer}} architecture with specialised transformers for each
concern (literals, expressions, patterns, statements), following the Single
Responsibility Principle.


\subsection{Consequences}
\label{\detokenize{adr/adr-001-lark-earley-parser:consequences}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Benefits:}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Earley handles all context\sphinxhyphen{}free grammars including ambiguous ones, so the
grammar can match the openCypher spec without workarounds

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Lark’s declarative grammar is readable and maintainable

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Eager background compilation means the parser is ready before the first
query in interactive sessions

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
The transformer architecture keeps grammar\sphinxhyphen{}to\sphinxhyphen{}AST conversion modular

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Trade\sphinxhyphen{}offs:}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Earley is slower than LALR for unambiguous grammars (microseconds vs
nanoseconds per parse); acceptable because query execution dominates

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Grammar compilation cost (\textasciitilde{}96ms) is paid once at import time

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Lark is a runtime dependency

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Constraints:}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Application code must use \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{get\_default\_parser()}} (the singleton) rather
than constructing \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{GrammarParser()}} directly

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Grammar changes require updating both the Lark grammar and the corresponding
transformer logic

\end{itemize}

\sphinxstepscope


\section{ADR\sphinxhyphen{}002: BindingFrame as Intermediate Representation}
\label{\detokenize{adr/adr-002-binding-frame-ir:adr-002-bindingframe-as-intermediate-representation}}\label{\detokenize{adr/adr-002-binding-frame-ir::doc}}\begin{quote}\begin{description}
\sphinxlineitem{Status}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Accepted

\sphinxlineitem{Date}
\sphinxAtStartPar
2024\sphinxhyphen{}06

\sphinxlineitem{Affects}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{packages/pycypher/src/pycypher/binding\_frame.py}},
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{packages/pycypher/src/pycypher/star.py}}

\end{description}\end{quote}


\subsection{Context}
\label{\detokenize{adr/adr-002-binding-frame-ir:context}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The original query execution pipeline used \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{Relation}} subclasses
(\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{EntityTable}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{Join}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{FilterRows}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{Projection}}) that operated
on pandas DataFrames with prefixed column names like \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{Person\_\_name}} and
opaque \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{HASH\_ID}} columns.  This caused three recurring problems:
\begin{enumerate}
\sphinxsetlistlabels{\arabic}{enumi}{enumii}{}{.}%
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Opaque column names} — \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{Projection}} produced \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{HASH\_ID}} columns
that required a \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{variable\_map}} lookup table to trace back to Cypher
variables.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Prefixed entity columns} — Column names like \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{Person\_\_name}} leaked
into operator logic, requiring hacks like \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{\_ensure\_full\_entity\_data}}.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Metadata threading} — \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{variable\_map}} and \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{variable\_type\_map}} had
to be threaded through every \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{Relation}} subclass, creating tight coupling.

\end{enumerate}


\subsection{Decision}
\label{\detokenize{adr/adr-002-binding-frame-ir:decision}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Introduce \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{BindingFrame}} as the intermediate representation for query
execution.  A BindingFrame is a pandas DataFrame where:
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Columns are named after Cypher variables} (e.g. \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{p}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{q}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{r}})

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Each row is one assignment} of entity/relationship IDs to those variables

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Attributes are never stored} in the frame; they are fetched on\sphinxhyphen{}demand
from \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{Context}} via \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{get\_property()}}

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
The column \sphinxstyleemphasis{is} the variable — no lookup table required.


\subsection{Consequences}
\label{\detokenize{adr/adr-002-binding-frame-ir:consequences}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Benefits:}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Eliminates the \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{variable\_map}}/\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{variable\_type\_map}} metadata entirely

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Column names are self\sphinxhyphen{}describing — debugging is straightforward

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Clean separation between structure (which IDs match) and content (what
properties those IDs have)

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Enables lazy attribute resolution — only fetch what RETURN actually needs

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Simplifies operator implementation (join, filter, project all work on
variable\sphinxhyphen{}named columns directly)

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Trade\sphinxhyphen{}offs:}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Property access requires a lookup into \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{Context}} rather than reading
from the DataFrame directly — adds one level of indirection

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Migrating from the Relation\sphinxhyphen{}based pipeline required touching most of the
execution path

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Performance notes:}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Module\sphinxhyphen{}level debug checks (set once at import) avoid per\sphinxhyphen{}call overhead

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Pre\sphinxhyphen{}built numpy ufuncs for ndarray normalisation

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{\_null\_series()}} uses \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{np.empty + fill}} instead of list allocation

\end{itemize}

\sphinxstepscope


\section{ADR\sphinxhyphen{}003: ID\sphinxhyphen{}Only Column Preservation Strategy}
\label{\detokenize{adr/adr-003-id-only-column-preservation:adr-003-id-only-column-preservation-strategy}}\label{\detokenize{adr/adr-003-id-only-column-preservation::doc}}\begin{quote}\begin{description}
\sphinxlineitem{Status}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Accepted

\sphinxlineitem{Date}
\sphinxAtStartPar
2024\sphinxhyphen{}06

\sphinxlineitem{Affects}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{packages/pycypher/src/pycypher/constants.py}},
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{packages/pycypher/src/pycypher/relational\_models.py}},
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{packages/pycypher/src/pycypher/binding\_frame.py}}

\end{description}\end{quote}


\subsection{Context}
\label{\detokenize{adr/adr-003-id-only-column-preservation:context}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
During query execution, the engine must track which entities and relationships
match a pattern (MATCH), filter them (WHERE), and then project attributes
(RETURN).  The naive approach — carrying all attributes through every
intermediate step — wastes memory and complicates joins when different entity
types have overlapping column names.


\subsection{Decision}
\label{\detokenize{adr/adr-003-id-only-column-preservation:decision}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Intermediate execution state (BindingFrames) stores \sphinxstylestrong{only identity columns}:
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{\_\_ID\_\_}} — unique entity identifier

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{\_\_SOURCE\_\_}} — relationship source node ID

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{\_\_TARGET\_\_}} — relationship target node ID

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
Attributes (\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{name}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{age}}, etc.) are fetched on\sphinxhyphen{}demand from the
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{Context}} layer only when needed — typically during RETURN projection or
WHERE property access.

\sphinxAtStartPar
This is enforced by convention: BindingFrame columns correspond to Cypher
variables and contain only ID values.


\subsection{Consequences}
\label{\detokenize{adr/adr-003-id-only-column-preservation:consequences}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Benefits:}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Joins operate on small ID\sphinxhyphen{}only frames, reducing memory and improving speed

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
No column name collisions between entity types during joins

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Clear separation of concerns: BindingFrame tracks \sphinxstyleemphasis{structure} (which IDs
match), Context provides \sphinxstyleemphasis{content} (what properties those IDs have)

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Lazy attribute resolution means unused properties are never fetched

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Trade\sphinxhyphen{}offs:}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Every property access in WHERE or RETURN requires a lookup into Context,
adding one indirection step per property reference

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Property caching is needed for repeated access to the same property within
a single query (handled by the property lookup cache)

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Constants:}

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{n}{ID\PYGZus{}COLUMN} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZus{}\PYGZus{}ID\PYGZus{}\PYGZus{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}
\PYG{n}{RELATIONSHIP\PYGZus{}SOURCE\PYGZus{}COLUMN} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZus{}\PYGZus{}SOURCE\PYGZus{}\PYGZus{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}
\PYG{n}{RELATIONSHIP\PYGZus{}TARGET\PYGZus{}COLUMN} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZus{}\PYGZus{}TARGET\PYGZus{}\PYGZus{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxstepscope


\section{ADR\sphinxhyphen{}004: Shadow\sphinxhyphen{}Write Mutation Pattern for Atomicity}
\label{\detokenize{adr/adr-004-shadow-write-mutations:adr-004-shadow-write-mutation-pattern-for-atomicity}}\label{\detokenize{adr/adr-004-shadow-write-mutations::doc}}\begin{quote}\begin{description}
\sphinxlineitem{Status}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Accepted

\sphinxlineitem{Date}
\sphinxAtStartPar
2024\sphinxhyphen{}08

\sphinxlineitem{Affects}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{packages/pycypher/src/pycypher/mutation\_engine.py}},
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{packages/pycypher/src/pycypher/relational\_models.py}}

\end{description}\end{quote}


\subsection{Context}
\label{\detokenize{adr/adr-004-shadow-write-mutations:context}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Cypher supports write operations (CREATE, SET, DELETE, MERGE, REMOVE) that
must be atomic — either all mutations in a query succeed, or none take effect.
A query like \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{MATCH (a) SET a.x = 1 SET a.y = a.x + 1}} must see consistent
state within the same query, and a failing query must leave the context
unchanged.

\sphinxAtStartPar
Without atomicity, a crash mid\sphinxhyphen{}query could leave entity tables in a
partially\sphinxhyphen{}mutated state with no way to recover.


\subsection{Decision}
\label{\detokenize{adr/adr-004-shadow-write-mutations:decision}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Implement a \sphinxstylestrong{two\sphinxhyphen{}phase shadow\sphinxhyphen{}write pattern} in \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{Context}}:
\begin{enumerate}
\sphinxsetlistlabels{\arabic}{enumi}{enumii}{}{.}%
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{begin\_query()} — opens a transaction by initialising an empty shadow
layer (\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{context.\_shadow = \{\}}})

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Mutation methods} (SET, CREATE, DELETE, etc.) write to the shadow layer
rather than modifying original DataFrames in place

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{commit\_query()} — promotes shadow DataFrames to the canonical
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{source\_obj}} on each affected EntityTable/RelationshipTable

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{rollback\_query()} — discards the shadow layer, leaving \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{source\_obj}}
unchanged

\end{enumerate}

\sphinxAtStartPar
All write\sphinxhyphen{}path operations go through \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{MutationEngine}}, which delegates
to Context’s shadow layer.


\subsection{Consequences}
\label{\detokenize{adr/adr-004-shadow-write-mutations:consequences}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Benefits:}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Full rollback semantics — failed queries never corrupt state

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Consistent read\sphinxhyphen{}your\sphinxhyphen{}writes within a single query (mutations read from
shadow when available)

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Simple implementation — shadow is a dict of entity\sphinxhyphen{}type to DataFrame

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
No external transaction coordinator needed

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Trade\sphinxhyphen{}offs:}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Memory overhead: shadow layer holds copies of modified DataFrames during
query execution

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Not suitable for concurrent multi\sphinxhyphen{}query transactions (single\sphinxhyphen{}writer model)

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Shadow merge on commit is O(n) in the number of modified entity types

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Lifecycle:}

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
begin\PYGZus{}query()
    ├── SET a.x = 1      → writes to \PYGZus{}shadow[\PYGZdq{}Person\PYGZdq{}]
    ├── SET a.y = a.x + 1 → reads from \PYGZus{}shadow, writes to \PYGZus{}shadow
    └── success?
        ├── yes → commit\PYGZus{}query()   → \PYGZus{}shadow → source\PYGZus{}obj
        └── no  → rollback\PYGZus{}query() → discard \PYGZus{}shadow
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxstepscope


\section{ADR\sphinxhyphen{}005: Backend Engine Protocol for Pluggable DataFrames}
\label{\detokenize{adr/adr-005-backend-engine-protocol:adr-005-backend-engine-protocol-for-pluggable-dataframes}}\label{\detokenize{adr/adr-005-backend-engine-protocol::doc}}\begin{quote}\begin{description}
\sphinxlineitem{Status}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Accepted

\sphinxlineitem{Date}
\sphinxAtStartPar
2024\sphinxhyphen{}10

\sphinxlineitem{Affects}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{packages/pycypher/src/pycypher/backend\_engine.py}},
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{packages/pycypher/src/pycypher/backends/}}

\end{description}\end{quote}


\subsection{Context}
\label{\detokenize{adr/adr-005-backend-engine-protocol:context}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
PyCypher was originally hard\sphinxhyphen{}coded to pandas for all DataFrame operations.
As datasets grow, users need alternative backends (DuckDB for analytical
queries, Polars for Arrow\sphinxhyphen{}native processing, Dask for distributed
computation) without rewriting the query engine.

\sphinxAtStartPar
Abstracting all \textasciitilde{}918 pandas API calls would be impractical and fragile.


\subsection{Decision}
\label{\detokenize{adr/adr-005-backend-engine-protocol:decision}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Define a \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{BackendEngine}} \sphinxstylestrong{protocol} that captures the \textasciitilde{}15 primitive
operations that BindingFrame, Star, and MutationEngine actually depend on.
Operations are grouped into five categories:
\begin{enumerate}
\sphinxsetlistlabels{\arabic}{enumi}{enumii}{}{.}%
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Scan} — load entity IDs from source data

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Transform} — filter, join, rename, concat, distinct, assign\_column,
drop\_columns

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Aggregate} — grouped or full\sphinxhyphen{}table aggregation

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Order} — sort, limit, skip

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Materialise} — to\_pandas, row\_count, is\_empty, memory\_estimate\_bytes

\end{enumerate}

\sphinxAtStartPar
Concrete implementations:
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{PandasBackend}} — default, zero\sphinxhyphen{}cost wrapper around existing pandas ops

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{DuckDBBackend}} — SQL\sphinxhyphen{}based analytical backend with lazy evaluation

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{PolarsBackend}} — Arrow\sphinxhyphen{}native backend

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
Backend selection is controlled via \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{PYCYPHER\_BACKEND}} environment variable
(\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{auto}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{pandas}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{duckdb}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{polars}}) or programmatically through
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{Context(backend=...)}}.


\subsection{Consequences}
\label{\detokenize{adr/adr-005-backend-engine-protocol:consequences}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Benefits:}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Users can switch backends without changing query code

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Protocol surface is small (\textasciitilde{}15 methods) and stable

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Each backend can optimise for its strengths (DuckDB for aggregations,
Polars for lazy evaluation)

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{auto}} mode selects the best available backend

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Trade\sphinxhyphen{}offs:}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Not all pandas features are available through the protocol; some advanced
operations may fall back to pandas

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Backend implementations must be kept in sync when new primitive operations
are added

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Testing must cover all backends to prevent drift

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Integration points:}

\sphinxAtStartPar
Operations that route through the backend:
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{BindingFrame.join}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{left\_join}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{cross\_join}}

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{BindingFrame.filter}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{rename}}

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{PatternMatcher}} — concat, distinct

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{MutationEngine}} — CREATE via concat, DELETE via filter

\end{itemize}

\sphinxstepscope


\section{ADR\sphinxhyphen{}006: Star Class Decomposition into Specialised Modules}
\label{\detokenize{adr/adr-006-star-class-decomposition:adr-006-star-class-decomposition-into-specialised-modules}}\label{\detokenize{adr/adr-006-star-class-decomposition::doc}}\begin{quote}\begin{description}
\sphinxlineitem{Status}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Accepted

\sphinxlineitem{Date}
\sphinxAtStartPar
2024\sphinxhyphen{}09

\sphinxlineitem{Affects}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{packages/pycypher/src/pycypher/star.py}},
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{packages/pycypher/src/pycypher/pattern\_matcher.py}},
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{packages/pycypher/src/pycypher/mutation\_engine.py}},
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{packages/pycypher/src/pycypher/path\_expander.py}}

\end{description}\end{quote}


\subsection{Context}
\label{\detokenize{adr/adr-006-star-class-decomposition:context}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{Star}} class was originally a monolithic god object responsible for
parsing, pattern matching, path expansion, mutation handling, result caching,
and query orchestration.  This made the class difficult to test in isolation,
hard to navigate (thousands of lines), and tightly coupled across concerns.


\subsection{Decision}
\label{\detokenize{adr/adr-006-star-class-decomposition:decision}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Extract specialised modules from \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{Star}} while keeping it as the single
entry point for query execution:
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{PatternMatcher} — translates MATCH patterns into BindingFrames via node
scanning, relationship joins, and multi\sphinxhyphen{}path merging

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{PathExpander} — handles variable\sphinxhyphen{}length BFS expansion for \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{{[}*min..max{]}}}
relationship patterns

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{MutationEngine} — all write\sphinxhyphen{}path operations (CREATE, SET, DELETE, MERGE,
FOREACH, REMOVE) with shadow\sphinxhyphen{}write atomicity

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{Star.execute\_query()}} remains the top\sphinxhyphen{}level orchestrator. Each Cypher
clause type dispatches to the appropriate module. \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{Star}} retains:
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Query parsing and clause sequencing

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Result caching (LRU with TTL and thread\sphinxhyphen{}safe locking)

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Context management

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
RETURN/WITH/ORDER BY/LIMIT projection

\end{itemize}


\subsection{Consequences}
\label{\detokenize{adr/adr-006-star-class-decomposition:consequences}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Benefits:}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Each module can be tested independently with mock Contexts

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Clear ownership: mutation bugs go to MutationEngine, pattern bugs go to
PatternMatcher

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Smaller files are easier to navigate and review

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Reduced risk of unintended coupling between read and write paths

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Trade\sphinxhyphen{}offs:}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Star still has substantial coordination logic and remains the largest
single file (\textasciitilde{}2,581 lines)

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Modules need access to Context and sometimes to each other (e.g.
MutationEngine may need PatternMatcher for MERGE)

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{2026\sphinxhyphen{}03 Assessment: No Further Decomposition Recommended}

\sphinxAtStartPar
A review (Task \#6) confirmed that Star now delegates to \sphinxstylestrong{7 specialised
engines}: MutationEngine, PatternMatcher, FrameJoiner, PathExpander,
ProjectionPlanner, AggregationPlanner, and ExpressionRenderer.  The
remaining \textasciitilde{}2,581 lines are orchestration logic (\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{execute\_query}} entry
point, clause dispatch, query planning coordination, timeout/cache
management).  This follows the \sphinxstylestrong{Mediator pattern} appropriately —
further splitting would scatter tightly\sphinxhyphen{}coupled execution flow across
files without improving testability, since each delegate is already
independently testable.

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Current delegates:}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{pattern\_matcher.py}} — MATCH execution

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{mutation\_engine.py}} — write path (CREATE, SET, DELETE, MERGE, FOREACH, REMOVE)

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{path\_expander.py}} — BFS expansion for variable\sphinxhyphen{}length paths

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{frame\_joiner.py}} — BindingFrame join operations

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{projection\_planner.py}} — RETURN/WITH projection

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{aggregation\_planner.py}} — aggregation dispatch

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{expression\_renderer.py}} — expression evaluation for display

\end{itemize}

\sphinxstepscope


\section{ADR\sphinxhyphen{}007: Extract Cardinality Estimator from Query Planner}
\label{\detokenize{adr/adr-007-cardinality-estimator-extraction:adr-007-extract-cardinality-estimator-from-query-planner}}\label{\detokenize{adr/adr-007-cardinality-estimator-extraction::doc}}\begin{quote}\begin{description}
\sphinxlineitem{Status}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Accepted

\sphinxlineitem{Date}
\sphinxAtStartPar
2026\sphinxhyphen{}03

\sphinxlineitem{Affects}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{packages/pycypher/src/pycypher/cardinality\_estimator.py}},
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{packages/pycypher/src/pycypher/query\_planner.py}}

\end{description}\end{quote}


\subsection{Context}
\label{\detokenize{adr/adr-007-cardinality-estimator-extraction:context}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{query\_planner.py}} module (1,360 lines) contained both query planning
logic and cardinality estimation primitives (\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{ColumnStatistics}},
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{TableStatistics}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{CardinalityFeedbackStore}}).  While there was no
actual bidirectional dependency with \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{relational\_models.py}} (as initially
suspected), the cardinality estimation code was tightly embedded in the
planner, making it difficult to reuse in other contexts (e.g. the query
plan analyser) or test independently.


\subsection{Decision}
\label{\detokenize{adr/adr-007-cardinality-estimator-extraction:decision}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Extract cardinality estimation into a dedicated \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{cardinality\_estimator.py}}
module (347 lines) containing:
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{ColumnStatistics} — per\sphinxhyphen{}column NDV, null fraction, histograms

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{TableStatistics} — lazily computed column statistics for a DataFrame

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{CardinalityFeedbackStore} — accumulates actual\sphinxhyphen{}vs\sphinxhyphen{}estimated ratios
for self\sphinxhyphen{}correcting estimates over time

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
All three classes are re\sphinxhyphen{}exported from \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{query\_planner.py}} for backward
compatibility.  \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{query\_planner.py}} reduced from 1,360 to 1,061 lines.


\subsection{Consequences}
\label{\detokenize{adr/adr-007-cardinality-estimator-extraction:consequences}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Benefits:}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Cardinality estimation is independently importable and testable

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Other components (query plan analyser, query learning) can import
statistics primitives without pulling in the full planner

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Clearer separation of concerns: planner decides \sphinxstyleemphasis{what} to do,
estimator provides \sphinxstyleemphasis{data} for those decisions

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Reduced cognitive load when working on either module

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Trade\sphinxhyphen{}offs:}
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
One additional module in the package

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Re\sphinxhyphen{}exports in \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{query\_planner.py}} add a small indirection for
existing callers (no code changes required)

\end{itemize}


\chapter{Overview}
\label{\detokenize{index:overview}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The project ships two packages:
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{PyCypher} — openCypher query parser, AST models, and BindingFrame
execution engine

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{Shared} — Common utilities, logging, and telemetry

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
PyCypher also includes \sphinxstylestrong{nmetl}, a command\sphinxhyphen{}line ETL tool that executes
Cypher queries defined in a YAML pipeline config.


\chapter{Key Features}
\label{\detokenize{index:key-features}}\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Complete openCypher grammar support (Earley parser via Lark)

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Strongly\sphinxhyphen{}typed AST models with Pydantic

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Vectorised query execution against pandas DataFrames

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
130+ built\sphinxhyphen{}in scalar functions

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Mutation support (CREATE, SET, DELETE, MERGE) with atomic rollback

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Query parameter injection for safe value binding

\end{itemize}


\chapter{Quick Start}
\label{\detokenize{index:quick-start}}

\PYG{k+kn}{from}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{n+nn}{pycypher}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{Star}
\PYG{k+kn}{from}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{n+nn}{pycypher}\PYG{n+nn}{.}\PYG{n+nn}{ingestion}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{k+kn}{import} \PYG{n}{ContextBuilder}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Build a context from plain DataFrames}
\PYG{n}{ctx} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{ContextBuilder}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{from\PYGZus{}dict}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{\PYGZob{}}
    \PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{Person}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{p}{:} \PYG{n}{pd}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{DataFrame}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{\PYGZob{}}
        \PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZus{}\PYGZus{}ID\PYGZus{}\PYGZus{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{p}{:}  \PYG{p}{[}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{p1}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{p2}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{p3}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{p}{]}\PYG{p}{,}
        \PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{name}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{p}{:}    \PYG{p}{[}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{Alice}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{Bob}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{Carol}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{p}{]}\PYG{p}{,}
        \PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{age}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{p}{:}     \PYG{p}{[}\PYG{l+m+mi}{30}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mi}{25}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+m+mi}{35}\PYG{p}{]}\PYG{p}{,}
    \PYG{p}{\PYGZcb{}}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{p}{,}
    \PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{KNOWS}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{p}{:} \PYG{n}{pd}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{DataFrame}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{p}{\PYGZob{}}
        \PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZus{}\PYGZus{}SOURCE\PYGZus{}\PYGZus{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{p}{:} \PYG{p}{[}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{p1}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{p2}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{p}{]}\PYG{p}{,}
        \PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZus{}\PYGZus{}TARGET\PYGZus{}\PYGZus{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{p}{:} \PYG{p}{[}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{p2}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{p3}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{p}{]}\PYG{p}{,}

\PYG{p}{\PYGZcb{}}\PYG{p}{)}

\PYG{c+c1}{\PYGZsh{} Execute Cypher — returns a pandas DataFrame}

    \PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}\PYG{l+s+s2}{MATCH (p:Person) WHERE p.age \PYGZgt{} 25 RETURN p.name AS name ORDER BY p.age}\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}}
\PYG{p}{)}

\chapter{Examples}
\label{\detokenize{index:examples}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{examples/}} directory contains runnable scripts that demonstrate
PyCypher features end\sphinxhyphen{}to\sphinxhyphen{}end:
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{``ast\_conversion\_example.py``} — Parse a Cypher query, convert to typed
AST, traverse and pretty\sphinxhyphen{}print the tree.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{``advanced\_grammar\_examples.py``} — Exercise the full breadth of the
openCypher grammar (OPTIONAL MATCH, UNWIND, CASE, list comprehensions,
variable\sphinxhyphen{}length paths, etc.).

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{``functions\_in\_where.py``} — Use scalar functions (\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{toUpper}},
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{toLower}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{trim}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{abs}}, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{toInteger}}) inside WHERE predicates
against a sample dataset.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxstylestrong{``scalar\_functions\_in\_with.py``} — Demonstrate scalar function usage
in WITH clause projections.

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
Run any example with:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
uv\PYG{+w}{ }run\PYG{+w}{ }python\PYG{+w}{ }examples/ast\PYGZus{}conversion\PYGZus{}example.py
\end{sphinxVerbatim}
